title: Barbecue Sauce on Cotton
stainChemistry: Barbecue sauce combines tomato (lycopene, tannins), sugar (caramelizes when set), vinegar, spices (turmeric/paprika pigments), and oil. On cotton, the sugar creates a sticky matrix that bonds pigment to fiber. Caramelized sugar from heat exposure becomes extremely adhesive. Multiple pigment sources (lycopene, turmeric, paprika) make this a persistent multi-colored stain.
whyThisWorks: Cold water dissolves the sugar matrix on cotton before it can caramelize. Surfactant addresses the oil component. Tannin spotter targets the tomato polyphenols. An oxidizer addresses the turmeric and paprika pigments. Treating quickly before the sugar sets is the single biggest factor in successful removal.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Remove excess solids with dull edge — do not rub
2. Apply Tannin Formula for the plant-pigment component
3. Apply POG for the oil component
4. Flush with NSD and cool water
5. If color persists: apply H2O2 6% on colorfast fabrics
6. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse. Blot dry

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden seam
homeSolutions.instruction: Barbecue sauce is a combo stain — tomato tannin, sugar, oil, vinegar, and turmeric/paprika pigments — so you'll use several cleaners in sequence. On an inside seam or hem, dab a drop of your Dawn solution and a drop of 3% hydrogen peroxide. Blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If no dye transfers and the fabric looks unchanged, you're safe. If the seam loses color, stop and go to step 8 (escalate).

Lift off any pooled sauce with a dull butter knife or the edge of a credit card, scraping away from the center of the stain. Don't press, don't rub, don't wipe in a circle — you're removing what's sitting on top so the chemistry below can reach the fiber. What looks like a small stain often has most of its volume sitting on the surface.

Turn the fabric inside out and run cold tap water through the back of the stain for 30–60 seconds. Push the sauce back out the way it came in. Cold matters here: barbecue sauce has sugar, and hot water caramelizes sugar into a glue-like film that holds the pigment to the fiber. On delicate items or upholstery that can't be flushed, dab the back with a cold wet cloth, then blot front and back.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — tackle the oil first
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab a small amount of clear Dawn dish soap straight onto the stain (no water yet). Gently work it into the fiber with your fingertips for 30 seconds. Dawn cuts through the oil component so the next step can reach the pigment. Let it sit five minutes, then blot with a cold damp cloth. Don't rinse it off yet.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide — tackle the pigment
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one teaspoon of Dawn with two tablespoons of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dab onto the remaining stain with a white cloth. Let it dwell 10 minutes. Peroxide oxidizes the tomato lycopene, the turmeric, and the paprika all at once. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Repeat up to two more times if a color shadow is still visible.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia or bleach. On brightly colored cotton, peroxide can lighten the dye — the step-1 patch test is your safeguard.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply an enzyme stain remover (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Shout Advanced) per the label. Let it dwell 20–30 minutes. Then launder in a cool or warm cycle (not hot) with your normal detergent. Cotton can tolerate warm water once the pigment is out, but keep it under 90°F until the stain is fully gone.

homeSolutions.agent: Air-dry and inspect under daylight — never tumble-dry early
homeSolutions.instruction: Hang or lay flat to air-dry, then inspect in daylight. A faint yellow shadow is usually turmeric residue — it often fades further in direct sunlight over a day or two. If you see any tan, orange, or yellow trace, repeat steps 5 and 6 before drying on heat. Only put it in the dryer when the stain is completely invisible in natural light.

If a yellow/orange halo survives two full rounds of peroxide + enzyme, or if the sauce was dried-on or heat-set before you started, take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Barbecue sauce on cotton — combo stain with turmeric residue. Treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + enzyme. No ammonia or chlorine bleach used." Pros use paint/oil/grease spotters and tannin-specific chemistry that outperform anything at home.

steps: 1. Remove solids with a spoon or dull edge — scrape, don't rub
2. Rinse with cold water to remove loose material
3. Apply Dawn dish soap. Work gently
4. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat if needed
5. Air dry — no heat until stain is gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 rounds of treatment. Combo stains are inherently harder — a professional cleaner has specialized chemistry for each component
warnings: Treat each component in order — don't skip steps
Cold water always — heat sets protein components permanently

neverDo: No prolonged acid exposure on colored cotton
No chlorine bleach on colored cotton — strips dyes
fiberSensitivities: Test colorfastness on colored cotton before peroxide

escalation.when: If barbecue sauce stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is barbecue sauce on cotton, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Can you identify the specific components?
Is there a greasy/oily feel to the stain?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
